title: Security Basics Every Dev Must Know
subtitle: SQL injection, XSS, CSRF, rate limiting, and OWASP Top 10

sections.title: SQL Injection
sections.text:
SQL injection is when an attacker puts SQL code into your input fields. Imagine a login form where the password field contains ' OR '1'='1 — if you're naively building SQL strings, this becomes SELECT * FROM users WHERE email = 'admin@site.com' AND password = '' OR '1'='1'. That returns every user. They're in.

The fix is simple and non-negotiable: parameterized queries (also called prepared statements). User input is treated as data, not code. Every database library supports this. ORMs handle it automatically.

Rule of thumb: never build SQL strings by concatenating user input. Ever.

annotations.text: Input treated as data, not code

sections.caption: Parameterized queries treat user input as data, never as executable SQL
sections.callout: SQL injection is the #1 web application vulnerability. Always use parameterized queries. ORMs help but raw SQL still needs preparation.

sections.title: XSS, CSRF & Common Attacks
sections.text:
XSS (Cross-Site Scripting) — Attacker injects JavaScript into your pages. Someone writes <script>steal(cookies)</script> in a comment, and every visitor runs it. Prevent by escaping all user-generated content and using Content-Security-Policy headers.

CSRF (Cross-Site Request Forgery) — Attacker tricks a logged-in user into clicking a link that performs an action (like changing their email). Prevent with CSRF tokens (every framework has this built-in) and SameSite cookies.

Rate Limiting — Limit how many requests one IP can make per minute. Without it, an attacker can try millions of passwords (brute force) or overwhelm your server (DDoS). Use the token bucket algorithm with Redis.

items.heading: XSS
items.body: Injected scripts. Prevent: escape output, CSP headers, sanitize HTML.

items.heading: CSRF
items.body: Cross-site request forgery. Prevent: CSRF tokens, SameSite cookies.

items.heading: Rate Limiting
items.body: Limit requests/IP. Token bucket algorithm. Use Redis for distributed limits.

items.heading: Clickjacking
items.body: Framing attacks. Prevent: X-Frame-Options: DENY header.

items.heading: Brute Force
items.body: Repeated login attempts. Prevent: rate limit + account lockout + CAPTCHA.

items.heading: Dependency Vulns
items.body: Vulnerable packages. Prevent: npm audit, Dependabot, lock files.

sections.caption: Six common security threats and how to prevent them

sections.title: OWASP Top 10 & Security Mindset
sections.text:
The OWASP Top 10 is the industry-standard list of web security risks. The top ones to know:

  1. Broken Access Control — Users accessing data they shouldn't. Check permissions on every request.
  2. Crypto Failures — Weak encryption, exposed secrets. Use HTTPS everywhere, never roll your own crypto.
  3. Injection — SQL, NoSQL, OS commands. Parameterize everything.
  4. Insecure Design — Security holes in architecture. Threat model early.
  5. Misconfiguration — Default passwords, verbose errors. Harden your setup.

Security is a mindset: trust nothing, validate everything, encrypt all traffic, hash all passwords, and keep dependencies updated. Use Dependabot, run npm audit, and enable 2FA on everything.
sections.caption: OWASP Top 10 — the most critical web application security risks
sections.callout: Subscribe to GitHub Dependabot alerts. Run npm audit / pip audit regularly. Use a secrets scanner (git-secrets, truffleHog). Enable 2FA on all accounts.
